questions.question: You specify a dependency as ^1.4.2 in your package.json. A new version 2.0.0 is published. Will npm install pick it up?
options: Yes, because ^ means 'any version'
No, because ^1.4.2 means >=1.4.2 <2.0.0, so 2.0.0 is excluded
Yes, because ^ only locks the minor and patch versions
Only if you also have a lockfile that references 2.0.0
questions.explanation: The caret (^) freezes the leftmost non-zero digit. For ^1.4.2, that's major version 1, so it accepts >=1.4.2 <2.0.0. Version 2.0.0 falls outside this range and will not be installed.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a lockfile (package-lock.json, Cargo.lock)?
options: To speed up package downloads by caching them locally
To record the exact resolved versions of all dependencies (including transitive) for reproducible installs
To declare which dependencies your project needs and their version ranges
To store your npm credentials and authentication tokens
questions.explanation: Lockfiles record the exact versions of every dependency in the resolved tree, including transitive dependencies. This ensures that npm ci or cargo build --locked produces the same result every time, unlike npm install which re-resolves from ranges.

questions.question: Your app depends on A@^1.0.0 and B@^1.0.0. A depends on C@^2.0.0 and B depends on C@~2.5.0. What version range of C satisfies both constraints?
options: >=2.0.0 <3.0.0 (the intersection of both ranges)
>=2.0.0 <2.5.0 (only the lower range matters)
This is unresolvable — the ranges do not intersect
questions.explanation: ^2.0.0 means >=2.0.0 <3.0.0. ~2.5.0 means >=2.5.0 <2.6.0. The intersection is >=2.5.0 <2.6.0. The resolver will pick the latest version in this range, e.g., 2.5.9.

questions.question: Why can npm install be non-deterministic without a lockfile?
options: Because npm downloads packages in random order
Because version ranges like ^ and ~ resolve to the latest matching version at install time, which may differ between installs
Because npm's SAT solver uses a random seed
Because package.json is regenerated on each install with different versions
questions.explanation: Without a lockfile, npm resolves version ranges like ^1.2.0 to the latest matching version at install time. If a new patch or minor version is published between installs, the resolved version changes. This makes the second install non-deterministic — same package.json, different node_modules.

questions.question: You discover an AGPL-3.0 licensed package in your project's transitive dependency tree. Your project is a proprietary SaaS application. What is the risk?
options: No risk — AGPL only applies if you distribute the software
AGPL requires you to make your entire application's source code available to any user who interacts with it over a network, which would make your proprietary code open source
AGPL only affects the specific package, so you can safely ignore it
You only need to add a license notice; no source code disclosure is needed
questions.explanation: AGPL-3.0 extends GPL's copyleft requirement to network use (not just distribution). If your SaaS application uses AGPL code, you must make the complete source code available to users who interact with it over the network. This is why AGPL is often called 'the GPL virus for SaaS.'

questions.question: Your project uses Go modules. Module A requires C@>=v1.2 and Module B requires C@>=v1.5. Go's minimal version selection (MVS) will resolve which version of C?
options: The latest version of C available (e.g., v1.9 if it exists)
v1.2, because it satisfies Module A's requirement first
v1.5, because MVS picks the maximum of all minimum version requirements
v1.7, because Go always picks a version in between the two requirements
questions.explanation: Go's minimal version selection picks the maximum of all minimum version requirements. Since Module A needs >=1.2 and Module B needs >=1.5, the maximum minimum is 1.5. MVS selects v1.5 (the minimum that satisfies all constraints), not the latest available version.
